next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

The kitchen in Martin Kessler's lake house had the sort of refrigerator that announced itself. Every forty minutes or so the compressor kicked on with a shudder that rattled the glasses in the cabinet above the sink. Mallick had timed it. Twenty years of police work had made her the kind of woman who timed refrigerators. She'd been sitting at the kitchen table since seven-thirty, and the machine had cycled four times, which put it close to ten o'clock, and she was nowhere.

Her leather notebook was open to a page that would have embarrassed her if any of her colleagues at the county office saw it. She'd drawn a grid. The columns were labeled with initials: PD, CH, NG, DW, TR, JB. The rows were labeled with the physical evidence. She'd filled in check marks, question marks, and, in two cases, small x's that meant confirmed absent. The grid looked like a badly designed crossword puzzle, and it told her less than she wanted and more than she expected.

She took off her reading glasses and set them on the table. The beaded chain caught on her coffee mug and she untangled it with the patience of a woman who'd been untangling the same chain from the same kinds of things for a decade.

Martin Kessler was dead of cardiac arrest. The medical examiner had been clear: hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, undiagnosed, aggravated by alcohol, sildenafil, and prolonged heat exposure from the hot tub. It was a natural death. People died like this. Healthy-looking men in their late forties dropped dead and their friends stood around looking stunned, and the medical explanation was usu...
open_hooks_to_carry: The group pushes Joel to explain the argument — the pressure has been building since Ch 4
Joel reveals: Martin was sleeping with his ex-wife Karen
The group's reaction treated like a fantasy football ethics debate — was it a violation? Is there a statute of limitations?
Paul's conflict: defending Martin while knowing Martin was wrong
Joel's catharsis — his anger finally has an audience and it is both genuine and absurd

payoffs_to_land.description: The argument from Ch 1 is explained — Martin was sleeping with Joel's ex-wife Karen. Joel's apparent suspiciousness dissolves as his motive (rage at a dead man) becomes sympathetically legible.

prev_tail_excerpt:
...id the fact that Tommy was twenty feet away on the deck, breathing hard enough that Cliff could hear it when the house noises settled.

He'd noticed pieces of it before and never set them side by side. Tommy going quiet any time the conversation drifted too far back. Tommy showing up to the draft every year no matter what else was happening in his life. Martin making sure Tommy kept showing up, calling him, covering the seat, smoothing over whatever needed smoothing. Cliff had always read that as ordinary loyalty. The letter in the safe stripped the ordinary off it.

Twenty-two years. Martin had carried Tommy's worst night for twenty-two years. Now Martin was dead and the thing that had kept that night buried was sitting in Cliff's pocket.

That changed the timing.

Before the study, Cliff had imagined two beers and a smooth opening. Now Tommy's silence looked different. Not embarrassment. Not grief by itself. His forearms were locked against the chair arms, his breathing too hard for sitting still. Cliff had spent twenty years reading clients across kitchen islands and closing tables. He knew panic when he saw it. Panic made people stupid. Shame that old might make them worse.

So not today. The conversation would happen, because forty thousand dollars was still forty thousand dollars and Cliff was still broke. But he was going to pick the moment with more care than he had first planned. No late-night deck talk. No springing it on Tommy cold. Not until Cliff had a better read on what those eight photos were worth and what they might cost the minute Tommy knew they existed.
secondary_character_beats: Tommy should weigh in with coaching-mode moral certainty that rings hollow given what the reader now suspects
Doug should say almost nothing but his face should register something — he understands being betrayed by someone close
state_deltas_required: the group demanding Joel explain his argument with Martin
the group's loyalty to Martin splinters; Joel's motive becomes sympathetic; the group fractures along new lines
